region_of_origin: India (North Indian / Mughal cuisine tradition); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'chicken tikka' class (FOODON_03302051). Pudina Murgh Tikka is a mint-marinated variant; mapped to the closest available class. No exact FoodOn term for mint-marinated chicken tikka exists as of April 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for tandoor-grilled marinated chicken. Pudina Murgh Tikka is a spiced, grilled chicken preparation; no more specific FoodEx2 code for tikka exists in the published matrix.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for chicken tikka or pudina murgh tikka. A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for pudina murgh tikka or chicken tikka as a prepared dish was identified in the published SNOMED CT release as of April 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Pudina Murgh Tikka is approximately 250 g, comprising 6 to 8 pieces of boneless tandoor-grilled chicken, typically served with mint chutney and sliced onion.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken tikka — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), adapted with mint-herb marinade adjustment from USDA FoodData Central entry for grilled chicken breast (FDC ID 171477)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides values for tandoori chicken / chicken tikka as a category. The mint-herb marinade (pudina, coriander, spices) contributes negligible macronutrient change at typical usage levels (<5% of dish weight). Core macros are taken from ICMR-NIN tandoori chicken values and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central grilled chicken breast (FDC 171477) for validation. Sodium is elevated slightly to reflect marinade salt content typical of restaurant preparation. Values represent a composite best estimate for the dish as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein-dominant grilled meat dishes — analogous estimate based on published GI values for tandoori chicken and grilled chicken preparations; University of Sydney GI Database reference for high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Pudina Murgh Tikka exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. GI is estimated at 30 based on the established principle that high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled meat dishes have low GI values (typically 20–40). Tandoori chicken and similar grilled chicken preparations are consistently classified as low-GI foods in peer-reviewed literature. The mint-herb marinade and spice blend do not materially alter the glycemic response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (30 × 2.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 2.1 g, confirming very low GL. This estimate is conservative and defensible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade and restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g restaurant serving of Pudina Murgh Tikka provides approximately 1050 mg sodium, representing about 46% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to 150 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pudina Murgh Tikka has an estimated GI of 30 and a glycemic load of approximately 2.1 per standard serving, making it a suitable high-protein, low-carbohydrate option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as naan or white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein source with moderate saturated fat and beneficial spice profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pudina Murgh Tikka is a lean grilled chicken dish with approximately 1.6 g saturated fat per 100 g; the mint, coriander, and spice marinade provides antioxidant compounds, and the tandoor cooking method avoids added frying fats, making it a heart-friendly protein choice when sodium intake is managed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pudina Murgh Tikka is a North Indian tandoor-grilled chicken dish in which boneless chicken pieces are marinated in a vibrant blend of fresh mint (pudina), coriander leaves, yoghurt, ginger, garlic, green chilli, cumin, garam masala, turmeric, and lemon juice before being skewered and cooked at high heat in a clay tandoor oven. The result is succulent, slightly charred chicken with a bright herbal flavour dominated by fresh mint and a gentle warmth from the spice blend. The dish is a variant of the classic Murgh Tikka, distinguished by the prominent use of pudina (mint), which imparts a cooling, aromatic quality that contrasts with the smoky char of the tandoor. Nutritionally, Pudina Murgh Tikka is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish providing approximately 165 kcal, 25 g protein, and 6 g fat per 100 g, with a low estimated glycemic index of 30. It is naturally gluten-free and suitable for halal diets. The dish is widely served in North Indian and Mughal-style restaurants across Singapore, India, and the South Asian diaspora. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic load, and for those following cardiovascular-friendly diets due to its lean protein profile and tandoor cooking method. Sodium content from the marinade warrants attention for individuals with hypertension.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Pudina Murgh Tikka exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN tandoori chicken / chicken tikka category values and cross-referenced with USDA grilled chicken breast data. The mint-herb marinade does not materially alter macronutrient composition. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database principles for high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled meat dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level measurement exists in an approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) tandoori chicken / chicken tikka category and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171477 (grilled chicken breast). No exact entry for Pudina Murgh Tikka exists in any approved primary source. 2. Sodium value (420 mg/100 g) reflects typical restaurant marinade salt levels and is higher than plain grilled chicken; individuals with hypertension should be advised accordingly. 3. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible in lean grilled chicken preparations per USDA data. 8. FoodOn match is closest_parent to FOODON_03302051 (chicken tikka); no exact FoodOn term for mint-marinated chicken tikka exists. 9. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) is the closest_parent defensible anchor; no dedicated FoodEx2 code for chicken tikka or pudina murgh tikka exists in the published matrix. 10. This dish is served at 3 Singapore restaurants on field survey (Bombay Grill, Bombay Curry And Kebabs, Aryana) at SGD 18.90–19.90 per portion.
